pi.github.com/users/adonath/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"open_issues":177,"closed_issues":4,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-09-02T14:05:54Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T13:26:38Z","due_on":"2025-05-30T07:00:00Z","closed_at":null},"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-27T16:59:07Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T13:49:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Gammapy version** 1.2 (but also tested with 1.0) **Bug description** Reprojecting a map in ICRS coordinates of given spatial binning gives weird binning patterns when reprojecting to another ICRS geometry when _preserve_counts = true_, depending on the binning. For our input binning of 0.1 deg per axis, reprojected pixel sizes of 0.05 deg or 0.025 deg work fine, whereas 0.06 deg (larger, so not really precision-factor-related) or 0.02 deg are problematic. **To Reproduce** See example code with example input map. **Other information** We’ve traced the issue to its origin; within reproject_to_geom the issue is caused by the input_map.resample call, and within this function it further points to the _resample_by_idx function. In our case that one is defined in /maps/wcs/ndmap.py. Here we discovered that the crucial part is the division of the weights by np.bincount in the if not preserve_counts call. And indeed, when setting preserve_counts to false the weird pattern does not appear (see test code). So it seems that the previous call weights = np.bincount(…) causes an uneven distribution of original bins in the weights within the final geometry, which needs to be corrected for by this division regardless if preserve_counts is true or false. In case preserve_counts is true, a subsequently applied scaling factor would probably do the trick; however it’s probably best if one of the core developers would have a look and check what the best way is (especially what this normalization factor should exactly be). [Binning_ExampleCodeAndMap.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17937707/Binning_ExampleCodeAndMap.zip) up on this, I'm now personally using the following change in _ndmap.py_, which seems to fix the issue: Instead of `if not preserve_counts` clause, I use the following: ` weights /= np.bincount(idx_inv).astype(self.data.dtype) if preserve_counts: weights *= np.sum(np.bincount(idx_inv).astype(self.data.dtype)) / len(weights) `","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/2523295979/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

version** 1.2 (but also tested with 1.0) **Bug description** Reprojecting a map in ICRS coordinates of given spatial binning gives weird binning patterns when reprojecting to another ICRS geometry when _preserve_counts = true_, depending on the binning. For our input binning of 0.1 deg per axis, reprojected pixel sizes of 0.05 deg or 0.025 deg work fine, whereas 0.06 deg (larger, so not really precision-factor-related) or 0.02 deg are problematic. **To Reproduce** See example code with example input map. **Other information** We’ve traced the issue to its origin; within reproject_to_geom the issue is caused by the input_map.resample call, and within this function it further points to the _resample_by_idx function. In our case that one is defined in /maps/wcs/ndmap.py. Here we discovered that the crucial part is the division of the weights by np.bincount in the if not preserve_counts call. And indeed, when setting preserve_counts to false the weird pattern does not appear (see test code). So it seems that the previous call weights = np.bincount(…) causes an uneven distribution of original bins in the weights within the final geometry, which needs to be corrected for by this division regardless if preserve_counts is true or false. In case preserve_counts is true, a subsequently applied scaling factor would probably do the trick; however it’s probably best if one of the core developers would have a look and check what the best way is (especially what this normalization factor should exactly be).
